author: @ZsOtherBrother
text:
I maybe wrong, but the 45° twist seems "backwards", which means it works to push the turbine counter-clockwise, *against* the blades' curves, that would have pushed the turbine clockwise... if it was designed to allow the wind to come from any direction other than directly from the front.
If I'm right, either the curve should be reversed, or the center twist should be reversed, i.e.: looking from the center towards the outer ring, the blades should be twisted clockwise, and then the outer edge of the blades should be twisted a bit further in the same direction to account for the difference in (linear) speed of the spinning blades' parts at different distances from the center.

Also, the center cylinder prevents the utilization of air flow through the center, so a simple cone, like the one you've used in some other designs, is probably a good idea too.

author: @cybercorp2000
text:
First, thank you so much for sharing these wonderful videos. Secondly, I was really intrigued by your earlier designs last year (around Nov 2020 - part 4), I think you were really on a better track then, with the VAWT. As I was looking at the design and listening to you talk about the lower wind speeds that are the reality of daily life, I thought a venturi increase, by extending  the side baffles and maybe adding a top baffle funnel the wind could dramatically increase the force applied to the blades. Mind you this is only an hypothesis but there have been dramatic increases in certain downtown buildings here in the US due to poorly though out architectural layouts of some high rise plazas. 
I think it would be really interesting to test larger baffles and try and induce such a venturi effect?

-Ron

author: @joedance14
text:
Interesting videos, and I agree with your conclusion regarding the role of the backplane.  However, that basically means these designs are “drag” type wind turbines - but the blades are aligned with the with the wind, generating no drag.  
Airfoil type wind turbines actually generate lift and “pull” themselves around, rather than being “pushed”.  That last design, with the open back bears a striking resemblance to modern turboprop propellers or advanced submarine propellers.  However, those have both an airfoil cross section, and a twist, designed to both catch the wind and generate lift.  
Thanks, and good luck.  
PS: wish I had a fraction of your skill in the shop!

author: @jajwarehouse1
text:
A few ideas for variations to test: 
1a. Have a cone or dome shaped solid backer and try with fins on the outside and the pointed end of the cone facing the wind.  The backer board may also not be attached to the blades, so that it does not need to spin.
1b. Have the fins on the inside with a hole in the middle of the cone/bowl (like the old round parachutes) and the pointed end facing away from the wind so that it catches the wind and funnels it towards the center to accelerate it.
2. Try with the original flat backer, but have the blades flipped around so that the taller part is toward the outside edge instead of the center.  Lay the whole thing horizontally so that it is omni directional.  A thin piece may be needed around the top edge of the circumference to stabilize the fins, preferably laid horizontally on the top of the fins so that it has as thin of a profile as possible.

author: @alexwild4350
text:
Hmm. There is the answer to one possible method of over speed control - if the back plane was moveable against the blades, then in an over wind/ over speed condition, a mechanical solenoid for example could cause the backplane to move away from the blades leaving a gap between blades and backplane thus lowering the efficiency - just what is needed when there is too much wind.
Still wasn't it Edison who said he knew thousands of ways to make a light bulb, but only a few of those ways actually worked. The Chinese proverb, "To the novice there are lots of ways forward. To the expert, there are few."
Learning by experimentation is never wasted. Knowing things that don't work moves you forward. Some day what didn't work becomes the exact answer later for something else.

author: @richardharvey1732
text:
Hi Robert-Murray Smith, good work!, it is so good to see someone prepared to take on such a project without too much forethought and design but there are some fundamental principles that must be observed, the first thing I saw at about four minutes thirty is that you have set up this 'open'  model with the vanes 'facing' backwards!, the whole concept relies entirely on presenting the 'blades' too the moving air at an appropriate angle, this angle has to be a percentage of the difference between the speed of the vane and the speed of the air.
At or new the centre of the device the speed at which the vane is rotating is slower than near the periphery, therefore the angle of attack should be steeper, out towards the tips of the vanes the angle needs to be much less because the speed at which that part of the vane is moving in a circular trajectory is much greater. There is a mathematical formula that describes this relationship. The product of any design is always a rotor that only works at its most efficient at one optimal air speed at either extreme the efficiency falls off. The only way out is to incorporate some form of pitch control but that has to include the option of variable twist in each vane.
The reason I can pass this advice to you is because I spent many days working on prototype wind powered dynamo devices to attach to the front of the bicycle that I rode to use some of the drag caused by my body to be absorbed by the turbine and charge the battery for night time use. We designed and built an eighteen inch diameter three-bladed propellor which did seem to work very well but was not robust enough. 
During this development we discovered that a serious limitation to our efforts relates to the fact that while the energy derived from the wind increases exponentially the load from the dynamo is linear so the thing would not work at all at very low speed at spun its nuts off at higher wind speeds!. What was required was an alternator that could vary the load by moderating the current to the field coils, all of which demands the application of far more skill and ability than I have access to.
There was on thing that emerged during the project that may be of use to you, if you cut sections of rotor vane from suitable diameter tubing you can incorporate the appropriate twist by including a radial component during cutting, resulting in vanes that un-twist as they extend from the centre. We did this with 65mm diameter plastic drain pipe for a rotor of about nine inch diameter for a wind speed of fifteen mph. I am sorry to say that I now cannot remember the mathematical formulae that we used but they are standard in engineering. Cheers, Richard

author: @zattan2002
text: Hi mate ! Here is a suggestion. The more space there is between blades, the more wind is lost. But depending on strength of the wind, the turbine will be more or less efficient. If the wind is weak, we need a sensitive turbine. So, the idea would be to use moving blades, constantly adjusting their angle, in order to get the more of the wind. If the wind is weak, the turbine is sensitive, but if the wind strengthen, the turbine adapts itself automatically. When the rotation speed increases, the angle of the blades changes, mechanically. So, the efficiency is always optimum. I don't know if it is possible. What do you think ? Cheers !

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: Cut a 50 gallon barrel or drum in half, take one half and mount it on one side of the blades then take the other half and mount it on the other side that the wind comes from. The side that the wind comes from will act as a tube tunnel and the closed side of the barrel will act as a tube wall which in turn will cause a vortex and help make it even faster. If you do this mate, you will be pleasantly shocked at the affect the two will have. It would had helped if the blades were smaller than the barrel tunnel but it will still work excellent mate.

replies.author: @jasonwitt8619
replies.text: If you do this, makes sure the barrel pieces are mounted only an inch or 2 from the blades, the closer the better without hitting.

author: @EdwardRLyons

This is an excellent example of the whole point of testing an idea. Does it work or not? If not, that in itself is a valuable thing. Even better is to work out *why* it doesn't work.
Anyone who doesn't agree with that view obviously doesn't understand the whole point of trial and error.

text: All that happened was that the rim wasn't strong enough to keep the circular shape and, by deforming downward, due to gravity, it became bottom-heavy. That's why it didn't spin.

replies.text: I know you probably already moved on from this like you always do but, with a stronger and lighter outer ring and some reinforcement of the blades so that it holds its shape you might actually have something.

author: @kenvater2843
text:
Dear Rob, love the channel(I am a member) but did you fall on your head with this one. There is so much wrong with this. you even forgot some of the original idea's like wind direction with a cone to direct it. Apart from everything wrong. you need to think of wind a bit like water, it has weight, it just work horizontally, as oppose to water and gravity downwards.

Had the wind been any stronger, it wouldn't have turned your turbine, it would rip it apart. 

The Wind like Water, will take the route of least resistant's, which includes the flexing of your blades, after all we don't make propellers out of elastic bands. and as for the cylinder you made to attach the blades too, you effectively made a brake. if you think of how a jet impellor is made, they spin in free wind when stood with the engines off and imagine how much weight is on a jet engine.

I hope this is of help, keep up the good work.

author: @petergreen1354
text: Have you thought of creating a wind tunnel design.  You can make long axle, short blade turbines that harness the wind from, as an example, the peak of a building’s roof and natural wind shear.  The small diameter of the blades are compensated by longer width. The wider the better. The key is to funnel wind energy directed by the roof effectively from both sides of the peak.

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text:
Hello Robert, didn’t take the time to read all the comments, but in this case- without the back plate- this should be the same as a conventional wind turbine. You chose the wrong twist of the blades. At the root of the blades you’re quite ok. But not at the outer rim. There the blades should be nearly in the same surface of the wheel instead of 90 degrees which means a huge airbrake. Think about moving your hand around you when you’re in a swimming pool. Keeping your hand flat or with a little angle of attack makes it plane over the water. A hand perpendicular to the plane of rotation gives huge turbulence and thus drag.
You can without any adaptations use a bicycle wheel and tape off the spokes simulating a windmill blade, in fact it is one

author: @NorthernKitty
text:
I was thinking of something in-between the flat and the open backing.  Like a child's pinwheel.  Or perhaps kind of a nautilus shape.  So the wind is blowing through/around a curve most/all the way through.  Just to increase efficiency some.  I'm thinking some energy is lost when it's being deflected at such a great angle as 90 degrees against the backplate.  I'm not at all sure if it's significant, but I have to imagine there's some loss there.

And, like you, I enjoy the failures as much as I enjoy the successes.  In fact, there's very often MORE to learn from failures - not just "what not to do", but sometimes you get unexpected results that apply to something else entirely.  Often, that's how the best discoveries are made - mistakes in the lab!!  😋😋😋

author: @williamfraser
text: You can get the best of both radial and axial flow by extending the blades past the outer edge of the backing plate and twisting only the protruding blade portion. Obviously this will not work against a wall.

author: @boblake2340
text: For the coils used with the rim magnets design, you really need a U-shaped iron core on which you wind the coil. The prongs of the U should be the width of the magnet spacing. The magnets should be laid with alternate polarity. This way, you increase the flux density through the coil, and the magnitude of the flux reversal. Finally, make the gap between the prongs and the magnets as small as possible.

author: @Kiyarose3999
text: Yes from the start I could see that the Air that is caught by the backplate would be lost instead of forcing the Air to the blades. The back plate is essential, the impeller design has been lost to much imo, and going back to the impeller but adapt it, rather than completely change its design. Sometimes you have to go back to go forward, perhaps this is one such time? I still think that the only thing that should of been changed to the impeller was to change the front plate for a dish/saucer shape like a Satelite dish. To direct more wind to the centre hole, with the blades cut so they widen to match the curve of the front plate. So making the impeller a bit like a Turbo charger, compressing the air that then expands as it follows the exit from the blades, I don’t know, I’m probably talking bs again, lol.

text: Love watching your channel.  How would you design a wind generator that would only be used with 30-60mph wind gusts?  We have conditions here that under wind warnings, the power company shuts off power to prevent fires.  It also happens to be during 105F weather.  We sometimes go without power for 2-3 days.  Do you have a design that would work, to give us power under those wind gust conditions to charge batteries, to keep a 600 watt kitchen fridge  and 100 watt fan running.  I'm thinking about LTO chemistry batteries that can handle high wattage charging from the sporadic gusts.

author: @kiddiescripterkiller

On the all Aluminum one... It's the Angle of Attack of the blades and amount of spring in the blades, they have to be more rigid. You loose power when they flex and it changes the AOA of the blades at the same time.
I would like to see the plywood backed one running horizontal to the ground.

author: @nil5221
text:
As has been stated many times in previous comments, a mistake was made with the 45° twist at the root; it's in the wrong direction. The root of the blade is driving in one direction, and the tip in the other . The blades need to continue the twist so that they get flatter (not more in-line) to the wind. The tip is moving much faster than the root, so this twist keeps the angle of attack constant; just look at any propeller.
However, I don't get the point: this all started as a possible way of overcoming Betz' limit (title of the first vid). This path (flow through a fan) has had billions of research dollars thrown at it already, over a century, starting from the Wright brothers, if not before. The simple value in this is to keep the backplate (bringing the velocity in the original airflow direction to zero), build your best possible generator, and see if you can possibly get power above Betz' limit (by simply checking mathematically). I think this was a distraction, it didn't really prove anything because of design flaws, and improving it will just make another fanblade limited by Betz' Law, at best.
Please put the backplate back on 😊 and keep going down the original experimental path

author: @leostarling5746
text: Those blades need to be propellor blades, with some profile to them to afford aerodynamic lift. Most of all, they must have much  flatter pitch. I.e. you need to turn them more than 45 degrees from your original design, till they are almost square onto the wind. Turn their tips more, not less, than at the hub. Then you will see it  go! Before you start cutting it up for scrap, just turn their tips full 90 degrees and attach them longitudinally along the outer ring. Then you will have achieved the long proven design of the Cretan Windmill.

author: @MadRat70
text: I think your structure is not rigid enough.  You might try something less complicated, like straight styrofoam arms coming off the central rotor.  RC airplane modelers have been using styrofoam for decades.  You only need a simple angle-L on the back and a chisel-point on the front.  The chisel-point minimizes drag along that side while the L-shape on the back picks up maximum drag not unlike your current project.  But it is a whole lot simpler to manufacture.

author: @montynorth3009
text:
The development of this design is unlikely to  bear fruit as its efficiency has worsened.
The original idea of reversing the function of a domestic vacuum cleaner centrifugal fan was a good one, but the suggestions of putting a large funnel over the hole to improve efficiency was turned down.
Why?
To say that the turbine turns perfectly well without a funnel is like saying that your car engine turns perfectly well on 1 cylinder.

replies.author: @ScarletFlames1
replies.text:
It will increase the maximum harvestable energy from the wind due to larger blade surface area, but it won't increase the amount of wind going through the turbine. This could actually be used for areas where traditional turbines are currently used as it will be able to be scaled up to harvest from higher windspeeds than the current design is able to.

The current design will simply ignore an increasingly larger percentage of wind once wind speed surpasses its maximum (which is pretty low for a wind turbine), which is part of why I think the open-faced turbine design is really cool as it does away with one of the biggest fail points for commercial wind-turbines, the brake system.


One of the drawbacks of what you're offering though is that an angle like that will allow some of the energy to not be imparted to the turbine due to the fact that it won't be forced to interact with the blades due to the back plate not blocking it. A small angle would probably be negligible but I want you to think on what result you'd get if you swept the angle of the back plate to be parallel with the central shaft.. It's just going closer to a traditional turbine design xD

replies.author: @Kiyarose3999
replies.text: @ScarletFlames1 If the front plate was retained but curved like a satellite dish, so most of the wind will be captured rather than bounce off a flat plate. Then the wind is directed into the centre hole and possible momentarily compressed? before expanding along the blades that would be wider as they get to the edge of the front plate following the curve of dish shape. So I’m talking about an impeller but just with a dish shaped front plate. Silly or. ?

Cos I think the impeller design is really good but cos air flow would  hit the flat front plate and bounce away. That a dish shape would catch most of that wind, and the blades would be wider as they reach the edge of the plate, which could possibly give it more centrifugal momentum?.

I have found with fans for windmills if they blow air with a motor they will work as a windmill.

author: @robertdeheer1307
text: What about mounting the turbine to the side of a building as you suggested earlier?

author: @carr60ick
text: Rob, perhaps keeping the backing but put holes in it...nothing too big to allow for a little back pressure...you can't have too many blades on an air screw/prop...the air needs to get through

author: @curtstacy779
text: My thoughts: you need to put the back panel on the 45-degree blades to see if there is a difference because we haven't ruled out if the 45-degree blades are what caused the problem. The 45 degrees almost looked like it created equal pressure on both sides of the blade. maybe not it's hard to tell without a good inline shot of the blades.

author: @Buzzhumma
text: The surface just didn't create enough resistance to the flow of the wind,. It makes sense in my mind that it is 50/50 balance. If the wind speed and volume is reduced by  50% after the collector then you have peak usability of the winds force. I am thinking about a sail boat just before it takes off unless of course your tacking a sailing boat which isn't a static object!

author: @gizmothewytchdoktor-419
text: has it been suggested yet to run these iterations horizontally instead of vertically?

replies.author: @martinwinfield2935
replies.text: Yes  I did was wondering the same thing. Then is doesn't matter which way the win blows.🌬

author: @friedrichtint488
text: Great video, thank you! :)

author: @YeshuaGod22
text:
it seems to me that this one would have performed much better if the angle at which the blades met the hub were flipped. The angle you've attached them at offers what I'd consider the back of the blade to the wind, but worse the circumferential hoop is directly in the path of the air that does follow the same path as air exiting mk2.
having said that, even remedying these issues wouldn't make it more interesting than mk2

author: @thomasmoore4576
text: Change the back plate to the shape of a cone and put the flutes on there kind of like a multi-fluided drill bit but fanned out like the center point and it won't be like hitting a brick wall when the wind hits it it was when it hits the point it spreads it and makes it spin up because it's got like a twist to the flutes like a drill bit as the wind hits it it will spin it up really fast really quick low wind speed trust me it'll work great

author: @mikewillemse3761
text: make it in a cone shape ... about 2 foot deep .it increase the speed of the airflow

replies.text: The whole thing and the backplate?. That could work. At least thats moving away from the direction of putting more gaps in it.

author: @nathancarmichael7050
text: We learn best from mistakes. Thank you have a wonderful day!

author: @kilokilos
text: Thanks, explains a lot, still going for a 2 x 1 meter fixed fan on the knock of the roof

author: @domesday1086
text: impellers tend to be optimized for high static pressure, the backboard converts air velocity to static pressure centered on, well, the center of the backboard. The only way for the pressure to equalize is flowing sideways down the blades. If you have a crosswind you get the same thing just with less wind area being captured, still totally functional. Either way though, the backboard is a necessary component. You are right that throwing that demonstrator together was a valuable test since that explanation would've just been a hunch and all sorts of ghostly unexpected things can happen in practice with fluid flows

author: @undernetjack
text: Next they will ask you to cant the blades, "then bob's your uncle"...a regular bladed wind turbine design...lol I am immensely enjoying the series Mate, Thank You. Also, it resembles a pinwheel, windspinner design I have seen, but it had two complete sets of counter rotating blades.
